title: From 2D Images to 3D Understanding
concept: 3D vision goes beyond per-pixel depth to build a full three-dimensional representation of a scene or object, enabling tasks like 3D reconstruction, robotic manipulation, and immersive AR/VR experiences.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can smartphone portrait mode typically rely on less precise monocular depth than a self-driving car needs?

options.text: Portrait blur tolerates rougher depth precision, while collision avoidance needs much higher accuracy
Smartphones physically cannot process any depth information
Self-driving cars never use depth information at all

exercises.explain: Yes! Different applications tolerate very different precision levels.

cards.title: Beyond a single depth map
cards.body: A depth map tells you distance from ONE camera viewpoint. But what if you want a full 3D model of an object you could rotate and view from ANY angle — like a product you could spin around on a screen? That requires going beyond single-view depth.

cards.title: 3D Vision
cards.body: 3D vision builds a complete three-dimensional representation of a scene or object — not just distance from one viewpoint, but a full spatial structure that can be viewed, measured, or manipulated from any angle, using representations like point clouds or neural 3D models.

cards.title: A single photo vs. a full clay sculpture
cards.body: A depth map from one camera angle is like a single photo of a statue — you see its shape from ONE side. Full 3D vision is like having the entire statue reconstructed in clay, which you can walk around and view from every possible angle.

cards.title: 3D product scanning for e-commerce
cards.body: A furniture company scans a sofa from multiple angles, reconstructing a full 3D model that customers can rotate and view from any direction in an online store — impossible with a single flat photo or even a single depth map.

cards.title: 3D vision isn't just 'more depth maps'
cards.body: It's tempting to think 3D vision is simply stacking many depth maps together. In reality, it requires solving a much harder problem: merging views from different angles into one CONSISTENT 3D structure, resolving conflicts and filling in occluded regions no single view could see.

cards.title: Single-View Depth vs. Full 3D Vision
cards.body: The scope difference between the two tasks:

left.text:
Distance from ONE camera angle
Only sees what's visible in that view
Cannot show hidden/occluded surfaces

right.text:
Complete spatial structure
Viewable from ANY angle
Can represent occluded surfaces too

exercises.prompt: What does full 3D vision provide beyond a single-view depth map?

options.text: A complete three-dimensional representation viewable and measurable from any angle, not just one camera's viewpoint
A higher resolution 2D color image only
A grayscale conversion of the depth map

exercises.explain: Correct! 3D vision goes beyond one viewpoint to build a complete spatial structure.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A single depth map alone already provides a complete, all-angle 3D representation of an object.
exercises.explain: False. A single depth map only captures distance from one viewpoint; it cannot show surfaces hidden from that view.

exercises.sentence: Full 3D vision requires merging views from different angles into one ___ 3D structure.
exercises.explain: Merging multiple views into a coherent, consistent 3D structure is the core challenge of 3D vision.

exercises.prompt: Why can't a single depth map alone let a customer virtually rotate a 3D-scanned sofa to see its back side?

options.text: A single depth map only captures distance information visible from one camera angle, not surfaces hidden from that view
Depth maps automatically include every hidden surface by default
Rotating a 3D model requires no additional information beyond one depth map

exercises.explain: Correct! Hidden surfaces need additional viewpoints or specialized 3D reconstruction techniques to represent.

exercises.prompt: An online furniture retailer wants customers to rotate and inspect a couch from every angle before buying, including the back and underside. What does this require beyond a single depth map from one camera?

options.text: A full 3D reconstruction merging multiple viewpoints (or a 3D scan) into one consistent 3D model covering all sides of the couch
A single depth map is always sufficient to show every angle of an object
This is impossible with any current computer vision technique
Only a 2D color photo is needed; depth is irrelevant here

exercises.explain: Exactly! Full rotation requires true 3D reconstruction, not just a single-view depth map. Next: the most common way to represent that 3D data — point clouds.
